data.content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me identify version conflicts and create the CPM setup:

- **System.Text.Json**: 10.0.1 (Api, Shared, Tests) vs 8.0.4 (Worker) → **10.0.1**
- **Azure.Identity**: 1.13.2 (Api) vs 1.11.4 (Shared) → **1.13.2**

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersions>true</ManagePackageVersions>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Azure.Identity" Version="1.13.2" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting" Version="1.15.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="10.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.3" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me identify all packages and their versions across projects:

| Package | Api | Worker | Shared | Tests |
|---------|-----|--------|--------|-------|
| System.Text.Json | 10.0.1 | 8.0.4 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 |
| Azure.Identity | 1.13.2 | - | 1.11.4 | - |
| Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting | - | 8.0.1 | - | - |
| OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting | - | 1.15.0 | - | - |


| xunit | - | - | - | 2.9.3 |

I've spotted version conflicts: System.Text.Json has 10.0.1 in most places but 8.0.4 in Worker, and Azure.Identity differs between 1.13.2 and 1.11.4. I'll resolve these by using the higher versions across the board. My plan is to create a centralized package management file at the solution root that enables version management, then populate it with all the package versions I've identified, removing individual version specifications from each project file.
